content.text: The attention mechanism is the fundamental computational primitive that enables transformers to model arbitrarily complex relationships between positions in a sequence — it is what gives LLMs the ability to understand context, resolve ambiguity, and reason across long-range dependencies in language.
At its core, attention is a

. Given a set of input representations, the attention mechanism computes a weighted sum of "value" vectors, where the weights are determined by the relevance of each position to a query. The key insight is that every output position can look at every input position simultaneously, and the degree to which it "attends" to each one is learned from data.

In transformers, this replaces the recurrence of RNNs entirely. Rather than processing a sequence token-by-token and compressing all past context into a fixed hidden state, attention reads the entire sequence at once and builds output representations that are informed by all positions — in a single, parallelizable operation.
The version of attention that dominates modern LLMs is
, introduced in "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017). It operates via three learned projections of each input:

Language is fundamentally relational. The meaning of any word depends on its context: "bank" means something different near "river" than near "loan." Resolving this requires comparing positions to each other in a content-sensitive way. Attention operationalizes this comparison directly.

Before transformers, sequence models relied on recurrence (LSTMs, GRUs) or convolution. Both suffer from a shared problem: information from distant positions must travel through many computational steps to influence each other, degrading gradients and losing long-range signal. Attention collapses this to a constant number of operations regardless of sequence length — every pair of positions is directly comparable.
This unlocks several capabilities that define modern LLMs:
: knowing that "it" in "the trophy didn't fit in the suitcase because it was too large" refers to "trophy," not "suitcase."
: correctly matching verbs to subjects separated by many intervening clauses.
: reasoning over examples provided in the prompt by attending back to them.
: attending to intermediate conclusions already generated in the context window.
How it works: Scaled Dot-Product Attention
-dimensional vector, the mechanism first projects every token into three spaces via learned weight matrices:

Q = X @ W_Q    # shape: (n, d_k)
K = X @ W_K    # shape: (n, d_k)
V = X @ W_V    # shape: (n, d_v)
The attention weight matrix is then computed as:
Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax( Q @ K^T / sqrt(d_k) ) @ V
Breaking this down step by step:
is the dot product between the query of position
is when constructing the output for position
. Dot products are a natural similarity metric: high value means Q and K vectors are aligned, i.e., the query finds that key relevant.
controls the magnitude of the dot products. Without scaling, large
values cause dot products to grow proportionally, pushing the softmax into regions with extremely small gradients (the softmax saturates near zero/one for large inputs). The
factor keeps the variance of the dot product at approximately 1 regardless of dimensionality, maintaining healthy gradient flow during training.
Applying softmax row-wise converts each row of the similarity matrix into a probability distribution over positions. The output for position
, which is always positive and sums to 1 across
. This is the attention weight: a soft, differentiable selection.

produces the output. Each output position receives a weighted average of all value vectors, where the weights are the attention probabilities. A position with high attention weight on another position copies more of that position's value representation into its output.
matrix where each row is a context-aware representation of the corresponding input position, informed by the entire sequence.

A single attention operation learns one type of relevance relationship at a time. But language involves many simultaneous relationship types: syntactic dependencies, semantic similarity, coreference, positional proximity, and so on. Multi-Head Attention (MHA) runs
independent attention operations in parallel, each with its own Q, K, V projections:

head_i = Attention(X @ W_Q_i, X @ W_K_i, X @ W_V_i)
MultiHead(X) = Concat(head_1, ..., head_h) @ W_O
Each head projects into a lower-dimensional space (
), so the total computation is the same as a single full-dimensional head. The output projection
recombines the heads into the original

The benefit is representational diversity: different heads can specialize in different relationship types. Empirically, individual heads in trained LLMs have been found to capture interpretable patterns — heads that attend to syntactic subjects, heads that attend to recent tokens, heads focused on coreference chains, and so on. The model is not told to create these specializations; they emerge from gradient descent.

attrs.alt: Scaled dot-product attention computation flow diagram

content.text: In decoder-only LLMs (GPT-style models like GPT-4, LLaMA, Claude), attention is
. This means that when computing the output for position
are attended to — future tokens are invisible.
This is implemented by adding a mask to the pre-softmax scores:

# mask is upper-triangular: -inf above the diagonal, 0 on and below
scores = Q @ K.T / (d_k ** 0.5)
scores = scores + mask           # -inf for future positions
weights = softmax(scores, dim=-1) # -inf → 0 after softmax
output = weights @ V

entries become exactly zero after softmax, effectively zeroing out future-position contributions. This ensures the model cannot "cheat" during training by looking at the token it is supposed to predict, and it means the same attention mechanism supports both training (parallel over all positions) and inference (autoregressive generation, one token at a time).
Positional Encodings and Their Interaction with Attention
A key subtlety: the raw attention mechanism is

. If you shuffle the input tokens, the output changes only in the corresponding permutation of positions — the model itself does not know which token came first. This is a problem for language, where order is semantically critical.
Transformers inject positional information either before attention (additive encodings) or inside the attention computation itself (relative or rotary encodings).
A fixed or learned vector is added to each token embedding before any layer. The sinusoidal encodings used in the original paper encode position

PE(pos, 2i)   = sin(pos / 10000^(2i / d_model))
PE(pos, 2i+1) = cos(pos / 10000^(2i / d_model))
Different frequencies at different dimensions allow the model to distinguish positions and potentially generalize to positions not seen in training.

Used in LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, and most modern open-source LLMs. Rather than adding a positional vector to the input, RoPE applies a rotation matrix to the Q and K vectors before the dot product. The rotation angle depends on position, so the dot product
naturally depends on the relative displacement
. This has proven superior for length generalization (extrapolating beyond training context lengths) and is the dominant approach in production LLMs today.
Adds a linear penalty to attention scores based on distance. Position
is a head-specific slope. This biases models toward attending to nearby tokens, which improves length generalization even further. Used in some models like BLOOM and MPT.
Key-Value Cache: Attention at Inference Time
During training, all tokens in the sequence are processed simultaneously — attention over the full
matrix. During autoregressive inference (generating one token at a time), recomputing Q, K, V for all past tokens at every step would be quadratic in time. The

At each generation step, the new token computes its Q, K, V. The K and V for the new token are appended to a cache storing all K and V values from previous steps. Attention is then computed using the full K and V cache, but only the new Q. This reduces inference complexity to
per new token (the cache grows linearly, and attention over it is linear in the number of cached positions).
The KV cache is the dominant consumer of GPU memory during LLM inference. For a model with
key/value dimensionality, and a sequence length of
KV cache memory = 2 × L × h × d_k × n × sizeof(dtype)

with 4096 context length, this is tens of gigabytes. Efficient KV cache management — quantization (int8/int4 KV), sliding window attention, paged attention (used in vLLM) — is one of the central engineering challenges in LLM serving.
Attention Patterns: What Heads Actually Learn
Empirical analysis of trained transformer attention heads reveals interpretable structure:
Many heads develop strong diagonal attention patterns, attending primarily to the few preceding tokens. These capture local context and syntactic adjacency.
Some heads attend nearly uniformly across all positions (entropy is high). These appear to aggregate global context, acting more like a "read the room" operation.

Heads that attend heavily to tokens identical or similar to the current token. These play a role in the "induction head" mechanism — a two-layer circuit where one head attends to the previous occurrence of the current token, and a subsequent head attends to the token that followed the previous occurrence. This gives transformers their ability to repeat or continue patterns seen earlier in the context.
Heads that track grammatical relationships — subject-verb agreement, determiner-noun pairs, preposition-object relationships. These emerge spontaneously without syntactic supervision.

In long-context models, some heads specialize in bridging widely separated positions, enabling the model to connect a question asked early in the context with a relevant passage thousands of tokens away.
Standard MHA replicates full Q, K, V projections for every head. This is expensive in memory bandwidth, especially during KV cache-heavy inference. Two optimizations are widely deployed:
All query heads share a single K and V projection. The KV cache size is reduced by a factor of
(number of heads). Used in PaLM, Falcon, early Gemini variants. Can degrade generation quality slightly relative to full MHA.
groups of query heads each share one K, V pair, where
. This is now the standard in most production LLMs — LLaMA 2 70B, LLaMA 3, Mistral, Qwen2, Gemma 2 all use GQA. It achieves near-MHA quality while drastically reducing inference memory and bandwidth.

# GQA: each KV group serves n_q/n_kv query heads
# LLaMA 3 8B: 32 query heads, 8 KV heads → group size 4
n_q  = 32   # query heads
n_kv = 8    # key/value heads
# Each KV head is shared by (n_q / n_kv) = 4 query heads
The naive attention implementation computes the full
score matrix and materializes it in HBM (high-bandwidth memory on GPU). For long sequences, this is the memory and compute bottleneck: an
(Dao et al., 2022; v2 2023; v3 2024) reorders the attention computation using

matrix is never fully materialized. Instead, it is computed in blocks that fit into SRAM (fast on-chip memory), significantly reducing HBM reads/writes. The result is identical to standard attention (exact, not approximate), but:
Throughput improves 2–4× on A100 GPUs for typical sequence lengths
Enables training and inference at sequence lengths of 32K–1M tokens that would otherwise OOM
FlashAttention is essentially ubiquitous in modern LLM training stacks (Megatron-LM, Hugging Face, llm.c, and virtually all finetuning frameworks).
Quadratic attention complexity in sequence length is a fundamental bottleneck. Several approaches attack it architecturally:
Each token attends only to a fixed window of
. Mistral 7B uses sliding window attention (window size 4096) with rolling KV cache, enabling efficient long-context inference.
Combine local windows with a small number of global tokens (like
) that attend to everything. This creates
complexity with retained ability to route global information.
Reformulates the softmax as a kernel approximation
evaluation order. Since matrix multiplication is associative,
can be precomputed as a fixed-size state, yielding
complexity. However, the kernel approximation introduces approximation error and typically underperforms standard attention on language modeling benchmarks.
An alternative research direction that forgoes attention entirely in favor of selective recurrence. Mamba achieves
inference state size, with competitive performance on many benchmarks, though it currently lags behind transformer attention on tasks that require explicit multi-hop lookups over long contexts.
In encoder-decoder architectures (T5, BART, original Transformer), the decoder includes
between self-attention and the FFN. Cross-attention differs from self-attention in that Q comes from the decoder state while K and V come from the encoder output:
CrossAttention(Q_dec, K_enc, V_enc) = softmax( Q_dec @ K_enc^T / sqrt(d_k) ) @ V_enc

This allows the decoder to selectively read from the encoder's representation of the input (e.g., the source language sentence in translation). The encoder processes the entire input with full bidirectional attention; the decoder can then attend to any position in that representation at each generation step.

Decoder-only LLMs (GPT, LLaMA, Claude) eliminate cross-attention entirely. Instead, the full context (instructions, retrieved documents, conversation history) is prepended to the generation target as a prefix, and the causal self-attention mechanism implicitly performs the same role as cross-attention through prefix conditioning.

The attention visualization above shows how different heads in a multi-head attention layer assign weights across positions. Each row corresponds to a query token and each column to a key token; darker colors indicate higher attention weight. Notice that some heads specialize in local patterns (adjacent tokens) while others attend broadly, capturing long-range semantic dependencies.

A common mental model error is treating attention as symmetric: "token A attends to token B" equally in both directions. In practice, the query-key asymmetry means that Q and K are projections into the same space and their dot product measures compatibility, but V is a separate projection — the actual information contributed. A token can be a "strong key" (easily found by queries) while its "value" contribution is minimal, or vice versa.
factor causes the dot products to be large when

is large, which pushes the softmax into very peaked distributions early in training. This causes vanishing gradients for most positions and effectively collapses attention to near-hard selection rather than soft weighting.
Assuming attention is interpretable by inspection.

Visualization of attention weights is popular but misleading. Attention weights do not equal importance — a token can have high attention weight but contribute little to the final prediction if its value vector happens to be near-orthogonal to what the next layer needs. Gradient-based attribution methods (integrated gradients, attention rollout) are more reliable for attribution analysis.
Assuming more heads is always better.

After a certain number of heads, additional heads typically contribute little — and in some models, up to 80% of heads can be pruned with negligible perplexity degradation. This has motivated research into head pruning and mixture-of-experts attention.

Attention's quadratic complexity means doubling sequence length quadruples attention compute and memory. A 128K-token context requires 1024× more memory in the attention layer than a 4K context at the same
. This is why KV cache memory dominates inference costs at long contexts.
Use FlashAttention in every training run.
It is exact (not approximate), faster, and memory-efficient. There is essentially no reason to use naive attention in a modern framework — PyTorch's

import torch.nn.functional as F

# Automatically uses FlashAttention if available on the hardware
output = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, attn_mask=causal_mask, dropout_p=0.0)
Prefer GQA over full MHA for large models.
The KV cache savings during inference are substantial, and the quality gap relative to MHA is marginal with sufficient model size and data.

Absolute sinusoidal or learned encodings generalize poorly beyond their training context length. RoPE, optionally extended with YaRN or NTK-scaling for length extrapolation, is the standard for production models.

Attention entropy measures how spread-out attention weights are. Heads that collapse to near-zero entropy (attending to a single position) are effectively inactive and a sign of training instability or redundancy.
A common bug is applying the causal mask as additive
. Multiplying by 0 does not suppress a logit — it must be replaced with
The compute cost of standard attention for sequence length

FLOPs ≈ 2 × b × h × n² × d_k   (for the Q@K^T product)
       + 2 × b × h × n² × d_v   (for the softmax(…) @ V product)
scaling is the central challenge for long-context models. In practice:
, the FFN dominates compute; attention is relatively cheap.
, attention memory (KV cache and intermediate matrices) becomes the binding constraint.
, FlashAttention or sparse attention patterns are required to stay within GPU memory budgets at reasonable batch sizes.
Serving a batch of 100 users with

context on a 70B model requires approximately 80–100GB of GPU memory for KV cache alone, before model weights. This is the primary reason that LLM serving infrastructure (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang) prioritizes KV cache quantization and paged memory management.

Research into what different attention heads actually learn reveals distinct, interpretable patterns that emerge during training. Understanding these patterns helps practitioners debug unexpected model behavior and design better prompts for agent tasks.

Early layers in a transformer tend to capture syntactic and positional patterns. Certain heads specialize in attending to the directly previous token (position-based), to matching punctuation (delimiter-based), or to the beginning of the sentence (global context tokens). Later layers develop more semantic patterns: coreference resolution (linking pronouns to antecedents), subject-verb agreement detection, and task-relevant associations such as relating a tool name in a schema to its usage in a prior example.
Diagonal heads — attend primarily to the current position itself (high self-attention weight). Common in shallow layers and when the token carries sufficient meaning without context
Previous-token heads — consistently attend to the token immediately before the current position, capturing local bigram statistics and dependency on the immediately preceding word

Broad global heads — distribute attention weight roughly uniformly across all positions. These heads aggregate sequence-level statistics and often respond to rare or unusual tokens that need broad context for disambiguation

Syntactic dependency heads — attend across distances to tokens in a syntactic relationship: subject to verb, adjective to noun, preposition to object. These emerge without any explicit syntactic supervision
Coreference heads — link pronouns (it, they, he, she) to their antecedents earlier in the sequence, which is especially important for multi-turn agents resolving references across conversation turns

Delimiter heads — attend strongly to special tokens such as [SEP], [CLS], or the beginning-of-sequence token. These provide the model with anchors for cross-segment retrieval in tasks involving multiple documents or structured inputs

For agent builders, these patterns translate into practical design guidelines. When the system prompt contains a long enumeration of tool schemas, attention must span across all those schemas to correctly select and parameterize a tool. Placing the most frequently used tools earlier in the schema list, or repeating key constraints near the task, can improve attention coverage. Similarly, when debugging failures where the model ignores instructions, visualizing attention weights on the relevant instruction tokens can reveal whether the problem is insufficient attention or incorrect downstream processing.

Building attention from scratch — beyond what the formulas show — solidifies intuition and is essential for custom architectures. The following example implements a complete bidirectional attention layer including projection matrices, a working forward pass, and an attention weight extraction utility:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math
from typing import Optional


class BidirectionalAttention(nn.Module):
    """
    Full bidirectional attention (encoder-style) with learnable
    Q, K, V projections and optional key-padding mask for variable-length batches.
    """

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, d_k: int, d_v: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.d_k = d_k
        self.W_q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_k, bias=False)
        self.W_k = nn.Linear(d_model, d_k, bias=False)
        self.W_v = nn.Linear(d_model, d_v, bias=False)
        self.W_o = nn.Linear(d_v, d_model, bias=False)
        self.attn_dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)
        self._last_weights: Optional[torch.Tensor] = None  # stored for inspection

    def forward(
        self,
        x: torch.Tensor,                         # (B, n, d_model)
        key_padding_mask: Optional[torch.Tensor] = None,  # (B, n), True = pad token
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        B, n, _ = x.shape

        Q = self.W_q(x)  # (B, n, d_k)
        K = self.W_k(x)  # (B, n, d_k)
        V = self.W_v(x)  # (B, n, d_v)

        # Raw attention scores
        scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(self.d_k)  # (B, n, n)

        # Apply key-padding mask (expand from (B, n) to (B, 1, n) for broadcasting)
        if key_padding_mask is not None:
            # key_padding_mask: True where the key position is a padding token
            scores = scores.masked_fill(
                key_padding_mask.unsqueeze(1),  # (B, 1, n)
                float("-inf")
            )

        weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)          # (B, n, n)
        weights = self.attn_dropout(weights)
        self._last_weights = weights.detach()        # save for visualization

        context = torch.matmul(weights, V)           # (B, n, d_v)
        return self.W_o(context)                     # (B, n, d_model)

    def get_attention_weights(self) -> Optional[torch.Tensor]:
        """Return the attention weights from the last forward pass."""
        return self._last_weights


# Example: batch with one padded sequence
d_model, d_k, d_v = 64, 32, 32
model = BidirectionalAttention(d_model, d_k, d_v)

batch_size, seq_len = 2, 5
x = torch.randn(batch_size, seq_len, d_model)

# Second sequence has padding in positions 3 and 4
pad_mask = torch.tensor([
    [False, False, False, False, False],  # no padding
    [False, False, False,  True,  True],  # last 2 are padding
])

out = model(x, key_padding_mask=pad_mask)
print(f"Output: {out.shape}")  # (2, 5, 64)

# Inspect attention: rows for the padded sequence should have zero weight on cols 3-4
weights = model.get_attention_weights()  # (2, 5, 5)
print(f"Weights seq2 col3: {weights[1, :, 3].tolist()}")  # should all be ~0

Attention weight extraction is a practical skill for auditing agent behavior. The following utility wraps a Hugging Face model and returns per-layer, per-head attention matrices without modifying the model itself:

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch


def extract_attention_weights(
    model_name: str,
    text: str,
    layer: int = 0,
    head: int = 0,
) -> tuple[list[str], torch.Tensor]:
    """
    Extract attention weights for a specific layer and head.

    Returns:
        tokens: list of decoded token strings
        weights: (seq_len, seq_len) attention weight tensor for the chosen head
    """
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    model = AutoModel.from_pretrained(model_name, output_attentions=True)
    model.eval()

    inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)

    # outputs.attentions: tuple of (1, num_heads, seq_len, seq_len) per layer
    attn_layer = outputs.attentions[layer]  # (1, num_heads, seq_len, seq_len)
    weights = attn_layer[0, head]           # (seq_len, seq_len)

    token_ids = inputs["input_ids"][0].tolist()
    tokens = [tokenizer.decode([tid]) for tid in token_ids]

    return tokens, weights


# Usage with a small BERT model
tokens, weights = extract_attention_weights(
    "bert-base-uncased",
    text="The cat sat on the mat.",
    layer=6,   # middle layer
    head=3,    # arbitrary head
)
print(f"Tokens: {tokens}")
print(f"Weight matrix shape: {weights.shape}")
# Weights shape: (9, 9) for 7 words + [CLS] + [SEP]

# Find which token 'cat' attends most to:
cat_idx = tokens.index(' cat') if ' cat' in tokens else 2
top_attn = weights[cat_idx].topk(3)
print(f"'cat' top-3 attended tokens: {[tokens[i] for i in top_attn.indices.tolist()]}")

In multi-turn agent conversations, the entire conversation history is concatenated into a single input sequence. Self-attention then operates over all turns simultaneously — the model has no special mechanism for 'remembering' earlier turns independently of later ones. Understanding this has direct implications for how agents behave.

Context length limits are hard cutoffs — once the conversation exceeds the model's maximum context window, earlier tokens are dropped. Unlike human memory, there is no summary or compression; the tokens are simply gone

Recency bias is real — empirically, tokens near the end of context receive more attention weight in many models. Instructions in the system prompt may be outweighted by recent user messages. Strategies include repeating critical instructions near the task or using structured formats that pull key constraints closer to the generation point

Tool results compete with instructions — when a large tool output (such as a JSON database record) is sandwiched between the system prompt and the next instruction, attention on the instruction may degrade. Keeping tool results concise and structured helps the attention mechanism connect instructions to outputs

The KV cache enables efficient multi-turn generation — in production, the transformer avoids recomputing attention over previous turns by caching the K and V projections. Each new turn only incurs the cost of attending the new tokens over the full cached history

Sparse context design improves reliability — removing boilerplate, repetitive content, and irrelevant tool results from the context improves attention signal-to-noise. A 10K token context focused on relevant information reliably outperforms a 50K token context with most of it irrelevant

From an architecture perspective, attention over multi-turn context works identically to attention over a single document — the model never 'knows' where turns begin and end unless turn delimiters are included in the token stream. The special tokens like [INST] in LLaMA or the role markers in chat templates (system/user/assistant) are the mechanism by which structure is communicated to the attention mechanism.
: each output position aggregates value vectors from all input positions, weighted by relevance computed via query-key dot products.
separates the "what I'm looking for" (Q), "what I contain" (K), and "what I contribute" (V) roles, enabling rich, asymmetric relationship modeling.
keeps gradients healthy by preventing dot-product magnitudes from growing with dimensionality.
runs multiple independent attention operations in parallel, allowing different heads to specialize in different relationship types (syntactic, semantic, local, global).
restricts decoder attention to past tokens, enabling autoregressive generation while preserving training parallelism.
encode relative position inside the attention dot product, making LLMs more robust to context lengths beyond those seen in training.
memory by tiling computation to avoid materializing the full
are the primary engineering levers for reducing inference memory and latency at long context lengths.
